abstract: This paper proposes a multi-modal approach to analyzing framing in political news communication, jointly considering textual, visual, and multi-modal cues rather than text alone with predefined frame sets. The authors apply discourse-analytic framing categories across modalities to a corpus of news articles about gun violence in the U.S., extracting both per-modality and cross-modal frames. They demonstrate how this richer framing extraction enables analyses of how outlets across the political spectrum differ in their editorial framing choices.
key_claims: Existing automated frame analysis is overly narrow because it relies on fixed predefined frames and ignores visual context.
News framing is inherently multi-modal, encompassing text, accompanying images, and their interaction, all of which reflect editorial choices.
Discourse-analytic framing categories can be operationalized and extracted automatically across modalities and their combinations.
Multi-modal framing analysis reveals systematic differences in how outlets across the political spectrum frame the same topic.
methods: Construction of a U.S. gun violence news corpus paired with accompanying images.
Automated extraction of frames separately from text, from images, and from text-image combinations.
Use of discourse-analytic framing categories rather than a fixed predefined frame inventory.
Comparative analysis of extracted frames across news outlets of differing political orientation.
findings: Frames can be reliably extracted at the textual, visual, and multi-modal levels using the proposed approach.
Different outlets exhibit distinct framing patterns across modalities when covering gun violence, varying with political leaning.
Visual and cross-modal cues capture editorial framing information that purely textual analysis misses.
contributions: Introduces a multi-modal framework for automated framing analysis of news that integrates text, images, and their interaction.
Moves beyond fixed frame typologies by leveraging discourse-analytic framing categories.
Provides a case study and corpus on U.S. gun violence coverage demonstrating the framework's analytical value.
Offers a methodological resource for computational social science research on political communication.
framing: The work positions itself within computational social science and NLP research on automated frame analysis of political communication, critiquing the field's reliance on text-only methods and predefined frame sets. It draws on discourse-analytic traditions of framing theory and extends them with multi-modal computational methods to better capture editorial decision-making in news media.
